title: 16_monthly_revenue.ipynb
descriptionMd:
## Mission: Where Did the Chart Go?

You wrote six lines of matplotlib to plot monthly revenue and your boss asked for the chart. You ran the cell — and nothing. No figure window, no embedded image, just text output saying `<Figure size 700x400 with 1 Axes>`.

That repr is your hint. matplotlib **built** the figure but never **rendered** it. In a notebook with `%matplotlib inline` the figure usually shows on its own at the end of the cell, but as soon as you assign or print the figure handle the auto-display is suppressed. Add the one missing call.
initialCode:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

months = ["Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "May", "Jun"]
revenue = [4200, 4500, 4900, 5100, 5400, 5800]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
ax.bar(months, revenue, color="#7CD992")
ax.set_title("monthly_revenue_2025.png")
ax.set_xlabel("month")
ax.set_ylabel("revenue (USD)")
print(fig)
traceback:
<Figure size 700x400 with 1 Axes>
# the figure built but never rendered — printing the handle suppressed the auto-display
correctOutput:
<Figure size 700x400 with 1 Axes>
# figure rendered below — bars climbing Jan → Jun, ~4200 → 5800

options.label: Call `plt.show()` at the end
options.patchCode:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

months = ["Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "May", "Jun"]
revenue = [4200, 4500, 4900, 5100, 5400, 5800]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
ax.bar(months, revenue, color="#7CD992")
ax.set_title("monthly_revenue_2025.png")
ax.set_xlabel("month")
ax.set_ylabel("revenue (USD)")
plt.show()
options.resultLog:
<Figure size 700x400 with 1 Axes>
# figure rendered below — bars climbing Jan → Jun, ~4200 → 5800
options.rationale: `plt.show()` flushes the active figure to the renderer (inline image in Jupyter, GUI window in a script). It's the explicit "draw what I built" call. The notebook's normal auto-display happens when the last expression of a cell *is* a figure; once you printed `fig`, the last expression was `None` (the return of `print`), and the auto-display didn't fire. `plt.show()` makes the rendering unconditional and is the safer habit.

options.label: Save the figure to disk so you can open it manually
options.patchCode:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

months = ["Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "May", "Jun"]
revenue = [4200, 4500, 4900, 5100, 5400, 5800]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
ax.bar(months, revenue, color="#7CD992")
ax.set_title("monthly_revenue_2025.png")
ax.set_xlabel("month")
ax.set_ylabel("revenue (USD)")
print(fig)
fig.savefig("/tmp/monthly_revenue_2025.png")
options.resultLog:
<Figure size 700x400 with 1 Axes>
# figure saved to /tmp/monthly_revenue_2025.png — but still no inline render
options.rationale: Writes the figure to disk, but the notebook still shows nothing inline — your boss is asking "where's the chart?" again. Saving is for shipping a final image; for showing it in the notebook, call `plt.show()` (and remove the `print(fig)` that suppresses auto-display).

options.label: Remove the `print(fig)` and trust the notebook auto-display
options.patchCode:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

months = ["Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "May", "Jun"]
revenue = [4200, 4500, 4900, 5100, 5400, 5800]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
ax.bar(months, revenue, color="#7CD992")
ax.set_title("monthly_revenue_2025.png")
ax.set_xlabel("month")
ax.set_ylabel("revenue (USD)")
options.resultLog: # (no output — relying on notebook auto-display, which doesn't trigger in scripts)
options.rationale: Often works in a Jupyter cell — the last expression `ax.set_ylabel(...)` evaluates to the Text object, not the figure, so auto-display is hit or miss. And in a plain `.py` script it does nothing. `plt.show()` is the rendering call that works in every context; reach for it.

hints: Read what the cell printed: `<Figure size 700x400 with 1 Axes>`. matplotlib built a figure but never *rendered* it.
Add `plt.show()` as the last line. That's the call that flushes the active figure to the inline display (or GUI window).
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
matplotlib separates **building** a figure (`subplots`, `bar`, `set_title`) from **rendering** it. In a Jupyter notebook the last expression of a cell is auto-displayed — *if* it happens to be a figure. Print the figure (or assign it, or call something that returns `None` last) and the auto-display step doesn't fire. The figure object exists in memory; you just never told matplotlib to show it.

### Why the fix is correct
`plt.show()` renders the active figure to whatever backend matplotlib is using — inline image in Jupyter, GUI window in a script, PNG bytes in a headless server. It's unconditional and explicit, so the chart appears regardless of what the rest of the cell does. The general habit: **build with `ax.*`, render with `plt.show()`**. It's also the call that flushes pending state so the next cell starts clean.
recruiterReview: Good — you recognised that matplotlib's figure-object repr is a smell, not a successful render, and reached for `plt.show()`. Two follow-ups: 1) in a notebook you can also write `display(fig)` or just leave `fig` as the last expression — but in scripts `plt.show()` is the portable answer. 2) When you have multiple figures in one cell, each needs its own `plt.show()` or you'll see them stacked oddly. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## How matplotlib Figures Get to the Screen

matplotlib has a build/render split that trips up almost every new analyst. Once you see it once, it never confuses you again.

### The two phases
```python
fig, ax = plt.subplots()    # build — creates the Figure and Axes objects
ax.bar(x, y)                # build — adds bars to the axes
ax.set_xlabel("x")          # build — sets a label
plt.show()                  # render — draws the figure where it can be seen
```
The first three lines don't show anything — they only configure objects in memory. `plt.show()` is the call that flushes them to the renderer.

### Why a Jupyter cell *sometimes* shows the chart anyway
Jupyter auto-displays the **last expression** of a cell. If that expression evaluates to a Figure (or anything with a `_repr_html_` / `_repr_png_`), Jupyter renders it. So
```python
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.bar(x, y)
fig                           # last expression — Figure object — auto-displays
```
works. But the moment you add a final line that returns something else (`print(fig)`, `ax.set_title(...)`, an assignment, an `import`), the auto-display chain breaks and you see nothing.

The fix is to be explicit:
```python
plt.show()
```
Now it doesn't matter what the last expression is — matplotlib renders whatever's pending.

### `plt.show()` vs `fig.show()` vs `display(fig)`
- `plt.show()` — renders the **currently active** figure(s) using the configured backend. In Jupyter that's inline; in a script, a GUI window.
- `fig.show()` — renders just `fig`. Useful when you have multiple figures.
- `display(fig)` — works in Jupyter (it's from IPython). Bypasses the auto-display chain.

In a notebook with `%matplotlib inline`, `plt.show()` is the most-portable choice.

### Saving vs showing
```python
fig.savefig("chart.png", dpi=150)
```
Writes the figure to disk — useful for reports — but **doesn't** show it inline. If you want both, do both.

### A small worked example
```python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

months = ["Jan", "Feb", "Mar"]
revenue = [42, 47, 51]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
ax.bar(months, revenue, color="#7CD992")
ax.set_title("Q1 revenue")
ax.set_xlabel("month")
ax.set_ylabel("revenue (k)")
plt.show()
```
The `plt.show()` is the line that makes the chart appear. Drop it and you have a beautifully configured invisible figure.

### The `%matplotlib inline` magic
A notebook line `%matplotlib inline` (often in the first cell) tells matplotlib to render inline images instead of opening GUI windows. It's the standard for Jupyter and is what makes `plt.show()` produce an embedded PNG.

### Common beginner mistakes
- Building a figure and forgetting `plt.show()` (the bug).
- Printing the figure and being surprised by `<Figure size ...>`.
- Calling `plt.show()` inside a `for` loop without `plt.close(fig)` afterwards — memory leak in long-running scripts.
- Mixing `plt.bar(...)` (state-machine API) with `ax.bar(...)` (object-oriented API). Pick one; OO is clearer in longer scripts.

### You just learned…
matplotlib **builds** then **renders**. `plt.show()` is the render call. In Jupyter, the auto-display chain rescues you when your last cell expression *is* the figure, but the moment something else takes that slot, the chart vanishes. Make `plt.show()` a habit and you'll never debug a missing figure again.

conceptCard:
This mission is about **how matplotlib gets a figure on screen**.

- `subplots()` / `ax.bar(...)` only **build** the figure in memory.
- `plt.show()` is the call that **renders** it to the notebook (or GUI).
- The Jupyter auto-display only fires when the last expression *is* the figure; `print(fig)` suppresses it.

The code prints the figure handle (`<Figure …>`) instead of rendering. Add `plt.show()` as the last line.

lineNotes.noteMd: Standard import — `plt` is the figure builder & renderer entry point.
Creates a Figure and an Axes — in memory only, nothing on screen yet.
Adds bars. Still in memory; the renderer hasn't been asked to draw.
**The bug.** `print(fig)` shows the repr string and breaks Jupyter's auto-display chain. Replace with `plt.show()`.

takeaway: Building a figure is not rendering it — end the cell with plt.show() to show the chart.
